items.question: Who uses cluster evaluation metrics in practice?
items.answer: Data scientists, ML engineers, and analysts evaluating customer segmentation, biological subtyping, or any exploratory grouping before deploying cluster-based rules or reporting research benchmarks.

items.question: What is the silhouette coefficient for a point?
items.answer: s(i) = (b(i) − a(i)) / max(a(i), b(i)), where a(i) is mean distance to same-cluster points and b(i) is mean distance to the nearest other cluster; the overall score averages s(i) over all points.

items.question: When should you use external metrics like ARI instead of silhouette?
items.answer: When ground-truth group labels exist for an evaluation set and you need to measure how well cluster assignments agree with those reference partitions, not when labels are entirely absent.

items.question: Where does inertia appear in a typical k-selection workflow?
items.answer: After fitting k-means for several k values, inertia is plotted on the y-axis against k on the x-axis; the elbow bend suggests where extra clusters add little tightness gain.

items.question: Why does inertia alone fail to pick k?
items.answer: Inertia decreases monotonically as k increases up to k = n (zero inertia when each point is its own cluster), so the minimum is trivially k = n and carries no useful structure signal.

items.question: How do you compute silhouette and inertia in scikit-learn?
items.answer: Fit KMeans and read `km.inertia_` for WCSS; pass scaled features and cluster labels to `sklearn.metrics.silhouette_score` (or `silhouette_samples` per point).

items.question: How does inertia compare to silhouette for choosing k?
items.answer: Inertia measures only within-cluster tightness and always favors larger k; silhouette balances tightness against separation to nearest other clusters and can peak at moderate k. Use both: elbow on inertia plus silhouette maximum in the candidate range.

items.question: How do ARI and NMI differ as external scores?
items.answer: ARI is chance-corrected pair agreement between partitions (can be negative, 1 is perfect); NMI is normalized mutual information in [0, 1] measuring shared information. Both need ground-truth labels; report both when benchmarking because they stress agreement differently.

items.question: What if you double k from 4 to 8 on the same dataset?
items.answer: Inertia will drop or stay flat (never increase), silhouette may rise then fall as tiny clusters form, and stability across seeds often worsens—re-evaluate per-cluster silhouettes before accepting the higher k.
